text: While animal models and static cell cultures (animal and human) of intestinal epithelial cells have been extensively employed to investigate the transport mechanism of heavy metal ions such as Hg(II), the small animal models do not truly reflect the intestinal condition of human being, and the cell cultures cannot in situ observe the cellular physiology. In the present work, a gut-on-a-chip device integrated with transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) sensors and electrochemical sensors was constructed to dynamically build up the physical intestinal barrier and in situ study transport and absorption behavior of heavy metal ions. The intestinal cell physiological environment was simulated by applying shear stress on intestinal cells (0.02 dyne/cm2), and cyclic tensile strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). The accumulation behavior of Hg(II) and the damage on cells at different Hg(II) concentrations in culture medium were detected by electrochemical sensors and TEER sensors respectively, which could accurately reflect heavy metal ions absorbed by intestinal cells and physical damage to the cells in vitro. Hg(II) absorption increased by 23.59% when the tensile strain elevated from 1% to 5%. Meanwhile, the expression of Piezo1 and DMT1 on the surface of intestinal cells increased after applying cyclic tensile strain.
